frameworks.name: optimal sensory encoding and decoding
frameworks.one_line: Normative encoder-decoder framework from which the law is derived

frameworks.name: psychophysical discrimination-bias relation
frameworks.one_line: Links discrimination threshold and perceptual bias as coupled, not independent

core_question: Is there a lawful mathematical relation between discrimination threshold and perceptual bias?
key_assumptions: Sensory encoding is optimal under resource constraints
Decoding is also optimal given the encoder
Discrimination threshold and perceptual bias have been treated as independent
manipulations: Examined psychophysical data spanning spatial context, temporal context, and attention
Compared predicted relation against a wide range of reported datasets
key_findings: Discrimination threshold and perceptual bias obey a simple, shared mathematical relation
The relation holds across context- and attention-induced perceptual changes
Wide empirical support across diverse psychophysical paradigms
interpretations: The threshold-bias relation reflects a general law of human perception
Optimal encoding-decoding principles can predict joint structure of perceptual measures
